feature: Words whitespace comments newlines and basic shell operators

feature: Single quotes double quotes and backslash quoting

Inside double quotes, backslash preserves ordinary backslashes before non-special characters and quotes representative special characters such as dollar sign, backquote, and backslash.

A backslash followed by newline is removed before token interpretation in unquoted and double-quoted representative contexts; kept out of the differential corpus because its runner decodes literal \n escapes.

feature: command substitution embedded in word token

Supported with POSIX backslash fixes but legacy nesting edge cases remain.
A legacy backquote command substitution is recognized and substituted into the surrounding word in a representative case.
Inside legacy backquote command substitution, a backslash before a non-special character is preserved in a representative case.
A legacy backquote command substitution inside double quotes preserves embedded IFS whitespace as one field in a representative case.

feature: Alias substitution timing and reserved-word interaction

feature: Alias values ending in blank keep the next word eligible for alias substitution

A <<- here-doc strips leading tab characters from body and delimiter lines in a representative case.

feature: Simple command assignments arguments and redirections

notes: Unsupported short options, unknown -o option names, and extra operands report diagnostics and stop non-interactive execution as special-builtin failures.
Baseline suppression and assignment command-substitution status exist but POSIX corner cases remain.
Unset parameter diagnostics, disabling nounset, and default-operator suppression are covered for representative non-interactive cases.
Disabling and re-enabling pathname expansion with -f and set -o noglob is covered for representative glob patterns.
Noclobber, set -o noclobber, append behavior, and >| override are covered for representative output redirections.
Output ordering remains baseline-only.
Simple commands and failed pipeline stage spawn report shell-visible errors.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors
notes: Strict mode has compound command, grouping, quote/substitution, redirection target, here-doc, and reserved-word diagnostics; recovery parser remains permissive for tooling.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors
notes: Special builtin expansion/redirection failures and readonly assignment consequences have negative coverage; broader exact exit behavior remains partial.

feature: IFS whitespace trims leading and trailing delimiters
notes: IFS whitespace at the beginning and end of expanded input is ignored for representative parameter expansion input.
Whitespace IFS behavior covers newline splitting and leading/trailing whitespace delimiter trimming.
Adjacent non-whitespace IFS delimiters produce an empty field for representative parameter expansion input.
Non-whitespace delimiter behavior covers comma, colon, and adjacent delimiter empty-field cases.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.6.5 Field Splitting; 2.6.7 Quote Removal
feature: generated empty field removal and quoted preservation
notes: Unquoted empty fields generated by parameter expansion are removed, while quoted empty fields are preserved as separate fields.

feature: literal IFS delimiters adjacent to expansions
notes: Literal IFS bytes that are part of the original word are preserved while expansion-produced delimiters split fields.
Empty IFS behavior has expected-output coverage showing splitting disabled.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.5.1 Positional Parameters; 2.6.5 Field Splitting
notes: Quoted $@ preserves separate fields, embedded prefix/suffix behavior, zero positional parameters, and empty positional parameters.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.5.1 Positional Parameters; 2.6.5 Field Splitting

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.5.1 Positional Parameters; 2.6.5 Field Splitting
notes: Unquoted $@ handles custom IFS, embedded text, zero positionals, and empty positional removal; unquoted $* custom IFS baseline is covered with dash/yash-compatible behavior.

feature: Default and alternate parameter expansion operators
notes: Default, alternate, length, and null-colon operator behavior is covered for representative set, unset, and null parameters.
The ${parameter:=word} assignment operator is covered for unset parameters and subsequent parameter visibility.
${parameter:?word} expands diagnostic words including unquoted spaces, reports the parameter name, and exits non-interactive execution for unset and null parameters.
Sorted results are covered for simple names.
Leading-period entries only match when the pattern component begins with a period.
Slash-separated components expand recursively and unmatched patterns remain literal.
Basic bracket ranges and negation are covered; locale-specific character classes and collation equivalence remain outside this row.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.13.1 Patterns Matching a Single Character; 2.6.6 Pathname Expansion
notes: Escaped glob metacharacters do not trigger pathname expansion.
Noclobber, >| override, and append interactions have corpus coverage.

notes: Bad input and output fd duplication report shell-visible errors. Kept out of differential scoring because dash differs on the command status for a bad input fd redirection.

notes: Bad input/output fd, noclobber, missing input, directory output, and command-not-found redirection diagnostics exist; ordered diagnostic fd duplication is covered in expected-output corpus; more redirection classes need stricter modeling.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors; 2.15 Special Built-In Utilities

notes: ${parameter:?word} and nounset expansion failures stop non-interactive execution for :, eval, export, readonly, set, unset, and trap.

feature: cd -L and -P logical versus physical PWD
notes: cd -L and cd -P cover symlink preservation/resolution, including logical dot-dot traversal.
Representative POSIX builtins accept -- as an option terminator before operands or no-operand operation, including after supported short options.

feature: cd logical dot-dot through symlink paths
notes: cd -L preserves logical symlink paths through .. traversal while cd -P resolves to the physical directory.
Unsupported options and excess operands report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.

feature: pwd -L and -P logical versus physical output
notes: pwd -L preserves the logical PWD path while pwd -P reports the physical cwd through a symlink.
Unsupported options and excess operands report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.
Slashless dot operands are found through PATH and evaluated in the current shell.
Sourced variables and functions remain in the current shell environment.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU break and continue utilities
feature: break and continue nested loop counts
notes: break n and continue n honor nested loop count operands for representative nested loops.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU break and continue utilities; XCU 2.9.4 Compound Commands
notes: Loops terminated or advanced by break and continue report the control builtin status without executing subsequent body commands.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU break and continue utilities
notes: break and continue outside loops report diagnostics and exit non-interactive execution as special-builtin failures.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU break and continue utilities
notes: Invalid numeric operands, zero loop counts, and excess operands report diagnostics and exit non-interactive execution.
exit invoked from a function terminates shell execution instead of returning to the caller.
exit uses the low 8 bits of nonnegative integer status operands in representative coverage.
Invalid numeric operands and extra operands report diagnostics and exit non-interactive execution.

Common file predicates include regular files, non-empty files, readability, executability, symlinks, terminal fd checks, setuid/setgid/sticky mode bits, character devices, named pipes, and portable block-device/socket false cases.

feature: test setuid setgid and sticky predicates
notes: -u, -g, and -k recognize set-user-ID, set-group-ID, and sticky permission bits.
-t fd reports whether a file descriptor is a terminal; corpus coverage verifies non-terminal false behavior and negative corpus covers invalid fd operands.
-L and -h recognize symbolic links while ordinary file predicates continue to follow links.
Character-device, named-pipe, block-device, and socket file type predicates are covered for portable true/false cases.
String equality and lexicographic ordering operators are covered for representative cases.
Integer equality inequality and ordering operators are covered.
Single non-empty and empty arguments, plus their negated forms, follow POSIX truthiness rules in test and bracket form.
String equality inequality non-empty and empty operators are covered.
test and [ support ! negation, -a, -o, and parenthesized expression grouping for representative expressions.
Invalid expression status 2 and diagnostic baseline are covered for a representative unsupported operator.

feature: shift default and explicit count operands
notes: shift without an operand shifts by one, explicit count operands update positional parameters, and function calls shift their local positional frame.
Overshifting reports a diagnostic and exits non-interactive execution as a special-builtin failure.
Invalid numeric operands and extra operands report diagnostics and exit non-interactive execution as special-builtin failures.
After OPTIND is reset to 1, getopts can rescan the positional parameters in a representative loop; kept out of differential corpus because dash differs for this reset-after-end case.
getopts stops scanning at -- and advances OPTIND past the terminator.
getopts uses explicit arg operands when provided rather than the shell positional parameters and updates OPTIND accordingly.
%b expands escape sequences from the argument and %c emits the first character of its operand.
Width, left adjustment, string precision, and zero-padded decimal formatting have corpus and differential coverage.
Octal and hexadecimal numeric conversions have corpus and differential coverage.
The format operand is reused until all operands are consumed.
read accepts -- as an option terminator both directly and after -r.
read accepts -r before -- and treats following names as operands.
Backslash continuation/removal and -r raw backslash behavior are covered for representative input.
With an empty IFS, read assigns the whole line to the first variable and empty strings to remaining variables for representative input.

feature: read field assignment with custom IFS
notes: Custom non-whitespace IFS and empty IFS field assignment behavior are covered for representative cases.

feature: read EOF status and empty assignment
notes: read returns non-zero at EOF while assigning an empty value to the requested variable.

feature: read assigns remaining fields to last variable
notes: When fewer variable operands than fields are supplied, read assigns the remaining fields and intervening delimiters to the last variable.
